segments.title: Historiophon’s 36-hour history-stream format
segments.summary: The Historiophon programme combines naval-history presentations, live questions, an essay showcase, and a World of Warships session across a 36-hour stream.
segments.body: This outline explains the event as an extended version of the channel’s live naval-history format: alternating question periods with focused presentations. The announced subjects include a ship-class primer, new-class submarines, and Admiral Jellicoe as Third Sea Lord, while the gaming session is framed as a way for viewers to take part together. Open this section to see how the long-form event was structured and which historical subjects it was intended to introduce.

evidence.note: The transcript lays out the alternating question sessions, presentations, essay coverage, World of Warships session, and the Jellicoe presentation.

segments.title: Jellicoe as Third Sea Lord and the interwar Navy
segments.summary: A planned presentation shifts attention from Jellicoe the fleet commander to his work as Third Sea Lord and its relevance to interwar naval development.
segments.body: The announcement identifies Jellicoe’s Third Sea Lord tenure as a useful case study for understanding what followed in the interwar Royal Navy. It points ahead to the connection with Henderson and the approach to the Second World War, rather than treating Jellicoe only as a wartime commander. This short preview is useful for viewers interested in the institutional and planning side of senior naval leadership.

evidence.note: The speaker introduces the planned Jellicoe-as-Third-Sea-Lord presentation and links it to interwar developments and Henderson.

segments.title: Why naval-history research should pay for archive images
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke explains why he pays for licensed archive images: evading rules set by large institutions can also harm smaller collections that rely on access fees.
segments.body: The argument treats archival images as property whose use should be paid for, even when a fee seems excessive. It stresses that normalising workarounds against large organisations can set a precedent that disadvantages smaller archives with modest charges and a genuine need for income. The point is a compact research-ethics lesson for viewers interested in how naval-history publications obtain and use visual sources.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the ethical case for paying image-access charges rather than bypassing an archive's criteria.
